business_ideas.idea: Ramp: corporate card + accounting software for businesses/creators (modern card for businesses, co-brand focus)
Manufactured housing: standardized, affordable home manufacturing addressing zoning constraints
Retail/creator co-branded credit cards (evolved from Paribus retail partnerships)

predictions.text: Banking industry will remain slow because it's anchored to 100-200 year old infrastructure and incentives

dynamics: Shaan does deep dive interview across Ramp's explosive growth trajectory ($1B/18 months), banking history, decision-making, emotional regulation. Eric articulate, reflective, grounds ambitious goals in philosophy (stoicism, history, parental lessons). Tone is cerebral; Shaan and Eric clearly have prior relationship/friendship.
frameworks: Reverse engineer from goal: set $1B/18 months, work backwards to 45-day, 60-day, 70-day milestones (network approval, bank approval, first transactions)
Velocity as moat: design company for speed; legacy competitors move at 1800s pace due to organizational debt
Extreme focus: choose 1-2 things, cut everything else ruthlessly; shipping features across many areas dilutes impact
Hire to offset weaknesses: identify what you're bad at, hire operationally unbelievable people in those areas (don't try to fix yourself)
Calendar/energy audits: ensure time spent matches what you want to be doing; reset weekly
Paranoia timeline: anticipate problems 3-6 months out, address before they manifest (vs. reactive problem-solving)
Post-acquisition integrity: build reputation for mutual wins; relationships compound across decades
learnings: Early milestones (45/60/70 days) reveal execution capability and founder discipline more than any other signal
Velocity as strategy: in stagnant industries, speed alone is a moat if execution stays high
Revenue growth from $10M to $100M doesn't require different genius; it requires staying focused and avoiding distraction
Hedonic adaptation: money removes existential anxiety but doesn't change baseline happiness; base anxiety returns once adapted
Credit is American cultural artifact rooted in 1900s consumer financing (cars, washing machines); credit acceptance is trust in upward mobility
Emotional regulation trained in childhood (conflict resolution exercises, mood observation with brother) compounds through life
Stoic philosophy (Ryan Holiday, Marcus Aurelius) teaches reset: can you choose not to be angry? Can you separate mood from decision-making?
History of banking shows compounding power: families that didn't sell through recessions (every 7-11 years) built dynasties
Compound 30% for 30 years > 100% for 1 year; design company for 50-100 year lifespan, not exit

banger_quotes.quote: It's best not to know the odds. You would never do it if you knew.
We wanted to go big or fail really quickly.
Most buildings in finance look like someone building a house with duct tape.
When things get risky, most people sell. These families just stayed in.
Compound 30% for 30 years and you'll be giant. That's not what we did, but we could.

numbers_dropped: $1B valuation: less than 2 years from incorporation
Time from $1M to $100M run rate: 15-17 months
Hiring rate at scale: 40-50 people per 2-week period
Days old: 2,310 days (6 years from incorporation to Aug 2025)
Paribus exit: mid-eight figures total (not per founder)
Market share: 1.5% of corporate/small business card market in US
notable: Eric's emotional regulation and calm demeanor despite extreme velocity is central narrative; credits childhood parental modeling (conflict resolution via empathy exercises) + later stoic philosophy. Explicit discussion of hedonic adaptation: once you have money, base unhappiness returns (still anxious, just existentially secure). Reverse engineering $1B/18 months goal into 45-60-70 day milestones is pattern-matching from Paribus failure (lost 75% revenue overnight due to unmanaged risk). Also distinctive: reading 15 biographies of Steve Jobs to study leadership evolution over career.
